host-2022-verb2-ELF-036.solution_path: The opening paragraph frames anthrozoology as having 'discovered a window into human sociality more broadly' — our interactions with animals work as 'useful models for understanding how issues of identity, nurturing, support and attachment play out in a relationship'. Bonds with pets clarify human nature. Answer C.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need the main argument of the OPENING paragraph specifically — not the whole article. 'Main argument' means the central claim the writer is selling, not a side fact.

steps.title: Locate the thesis sentence
steps.text: The opening paragraph ends with two parallel claims: anthrozoologists found 'a window into human sociality' and 'Pets help us fill our need for social connectedness.' Both point outward from pets toward human psychology.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: By watching how people relate to their pets, scientists learn how people relate to other people. The pets are the LENS; humans are the SUBJECT.

steps.title: Match the paraphrase to the options
steps.text: Option C says 'Bonds between pets and people provide clues to parts of human nature' — that is exactly the lens-on-humans framing. The bond is the source of clues; human nature is what we learn about.

steps.title: Verify against the paragraph
steps.text: Re-read: 'It's all about human psychology... pets help us fill our need for social connectedness... the more we uncover about our companion animals, the more we may learn about our human attachments.' Every sentence confirms the human-self-understanding frame.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer C. Insight in one line: in an ELF main-idea question, follow the direction the writer points — if pets are framed as the lens for human psychology, the answer names the human-side payoff.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to remember the later phrase 'still in its infancy' and read it as 'largely unexplored'.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 catches it: the opening explicitly says psychologists have spent 50 years on this. 'Infancy' refers to a young field that is being actively explored, not absent territory.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the comparative framing and assume the paragraph is about animal-human behavioural parallels.
distractors.why_wrong: As step 3 shows, the paragraph treats pets as a window onto HUMAN sociality — not as evidence that animals and humans behave alike. The direction of the claim runs from pets to humans, not between them.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a companion-animal article is to expect a 'mental closeness' thesis.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 names the actual thesis: bonds reveal HUMAN attachment patterns. Nothing in the opening paragraph claims that human and animal minds are more similar than commonly thought.

host-2022-verb2-ELF-036.technique: ELF main-idea questions almost always reward the option that copies the writer's directional move. Here the writer turns from pets to humans; the right answer must reproduce that turn.
host-2022-verb2-ELF-036.pitfall: The cure for the 'animal-behavior parallel' trap: ask which side the writer is treating as evidence and which side as the target. Pets here are evidence; humans are the target — so pick the option that says 'pets tell us about humans', never one that says 'pets and humans behave alike'.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the opening frames topic X as a way to understand topic Y, pick the option whose subject is Y and whose evidence is X — never the symmetric option that treats X and Y as parallel.

host-2022-verb2-ELF-037.solution_path: The passage stacks three pieces of biological evidence: toddlers prefer live animals to toys, the amygdala has neurons that respond preferentially to animal images, and humans share an inborn response to infant-like features that baby animals exploit. Together these say humans are biologically primed for a positive response to animals. Answer A.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need the option that the text most strongly supports — not a plausible extension, but a statement the writer would sign their name to.

steps.title: Find the evidence trio
steps.text: Locate the second paragraph: 'Part of our attraction to animal companionship is innate.' That sentence is the topic claim. Three pieces of support follow: (i) toddlers prefer animals to toys, (ii) amygdala neurons fire for animal images, (iii) baby-animal features trigger the same instincts as human infants.

steps.title: Paraphrase the topic claim
steps.text: 'Innate' means built-in, hard-wired, present from birth. 'Wired with a favorable attitude' in option A is the everyday English version of the same idea.

steps.title: Note the hedge — 'to some extent'
steps.text: Option A says 'to some extent', not 'entirely'. That matches the text exactly: the writer says 'PART of our attraction is innate' and later acknowledges culture also matters. The hedge is the option's safety bolt.

steps.title: Verify against the options
steps.text: Re-read 'a neural basis for the powerful emotional reactions animals elicit from us' — that is option A almost verbatim. The other options each over-reach in a specific direction; step 6 lists the traps.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer A. Insight in one line: in ELF agreement questions, the option that hedges ('to some extent', 'often', 'may') usually wins, because passages rarely make absolute claims.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'baby animals exploit the same instinctive responses... as human infants' as 'humans treat infants and animals similarly across cultures'.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 traces the claim: the shared trigger is in OUR brains, not in cultural treatment. The passage even says dogs are unclean in some Islamic cultures — so cross-cultural similarity of treatment is explicitly denied.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'specialized brain cells' and infer that the brain ADAPTED to animal company over time.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 fixes the meaning: 'innate' and 'inborn' are present-tense facts, not claims about gradual adaptation. The passage never describes evolutionary change — it describes an already-existing mechanism.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'protective instincts' phrasing is reciprocal — humans protect, animals respond.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 5 catches it: every sentence describes the HUMAN side of the interaction (toddler choice, human neurons, human nurturing instincts). Animal reactions to us are simply not the topic.

host-2022-verb2-ELF-037.technique: When a paragraph opens with 'X is innate' and follows with neural, developmental and instinctive evidence, the matching option will mirror that hedged biological framing, usually with a 'to some extent' qualifier.
host-2022-verb2-ELF-037.pitfall: The cure for 'over-confident' distractors: scan each option for absolute words ('always', 'identical', 'in general', 'most cultures') and compare to the passage's actual hedge ('part of', 'may', 'some'). When the option's certainty exceeds the passage's certainty, it is wrong even if the topic matches.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the passage hedges with 'part of', 'may', 'some' and the option hedges with 'to some extent', 'often' — trust the match; over-confident options lose.

host-2022-verb2-ELF-038.solution_path: Wilson is associated with 'biophilia' — an innate human pull toward life — but he also 'acknowledged that our interest in animals depends on personal and cultural experience'. Herzog argues 'pet keeping is driven principally by culture'. So Wilson puts innate factors first and culture second; Herzog flips that order, with culture primary. Answer D.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need the exact mapping of two named researchers to two positions. Wrong distractors will swap them, exaggerate them, or smush them together.

steps.title: Pin down Wilson's view
steps.text: Wilson is the 'biophilia' guy — an 'inherent tendency to focus on life and lifelike processes'. Inherent = innate. But the next sentence is critical: 'However, Wilson has also acknowledged that our interest in animals depends on personal and cultural experience.' He doesn't deny culture — he just makes it the second slot.

steps.title: Pin down Herzog's view
steps.text: Herzog 'has argued that pet keeping is driven principally by culture'. 'Principally' means primarily, mostly. He then supports this with the fluctuating dog-breed popularity that tracks fashion rather than the dog's traits.

steps.title: Build the mapping
steps.text: Wilson: innate first, culture second. Herzog: culture first, innate second (he even extends to all species via social imitation). Both accept both factors — they only disagree on the RANKING.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.text: Option D says 'Herzog sees culture as primary; Wilson regards culture as secondary' — that captures the ranking flip exactly. No option says 'Wilson sees innate as primary' literally, but D's statement about Wilson treating culture as secondary is the logical mirror.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer D. Insight in one line: in two-researcher contrast questions, the right answer almost always describes a RANKING DIFFERENCE, not a total opposition — both researchers usually share most of the framework.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read Wilson's 'biophilia' as denying culture, and Herzog's culture focus as denying genes.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 caught Wilson's explicit concession to culture, and Herzog's argument is about 'principally', not 'entirely' — neither man denies the other factor. The option overstates both positions.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the contrast structure and assume the two are 'opposite extremes'.
distractors.why_wrong: As step 4 shows, neither researcher takes an absolute position. The phrase 'innate factors alone are decisive' in B doesn't match Wilson, who explicitly conceded a role for culture.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a nature/nurture pair is to declare them balanced.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 5 traces the rankings: the passage's whole point in setting up the two researchers is that they DISAGREE on which factor leads. 'Equally important' erases the disagreement the passage is building.

host-2022-verb2-ELF-038.technique: ELF two-researcher questions test whether you noticed the RANKING difference. Both will usually accept both factors; what differs is which one each puts first. Search for the word 'principally' or 'primarily' next to one researcher's view to anchor the ranking.
host-2022-verb2-ELF-038.pitfall: The cure for 'extreme-opposite' trap: never accept an option that uses 'alone', 'only', 'denies', or 'excludes' unless the passage uses one of those words too. Researchers in HP ELF passages almost always allow the other factor — they just rank it lower.

pregrade_tactic.move: Find the word that ranks each researcher's preferred factor ('principally', 'mainly', 'inherent') — the right option will describe the FLIP of those rankings, never a full opposition.

host-2022-verb2-ELF-039.solution_path: The passage describes the 1980 Friedmann finding (pet owners survived heart attacks better) and immediately undercuts it: 'efforts to replicate such findings have had mixed results, and animal-assisted therapy has been criticized for overstating the ability of animals to ameliorate mental illness.' That is the canonical 'we don't know yet' verdict. Answer B.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need the passage's verdict on whether pets give psychological or medical benefits. Note the word 'concerning' — it asks what is SAID, so look for the writer's framing, not your own opinion.

steps.title: Locate the medical paragraph
steps.text: Find Friedmann 1980: pet owners 'were more likely to be alive a year after a heart attack'. That is the positive finding. Then the pivot: 'Yet efforts to replicate such findings have had mixed results'. The word 'Yet' signals the writer's hedge.

steps.title: Read the hedge in plain English
steps.text: 'Mixed results' = some studies confirm, some don't. 'Animal-assisted therapy has been criticized for overstating' = the benefit might be smaller than claimed. 'Nevertheless, some people may gain psychological support' = a partial concession. Translation: the benefit is uncertain, neither confirmed nor denied.

steps.title: Match the verdict to options
steps.text: Option B says 'It is unclear whether owning a pet gives a health advantage' — that is the literal meaning of 'mixed results' and 'overstating'. The word 'unclear' is the exact register the passage uses.

steps.title: Verify
steps.text: Re-read 'efforts to replicate... have had mixed results' — replication failure is the textbook indicator of unclear evidence. Option B owns that meaning.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer B. Insight in one line: when an ELF passage states a positive finding and then says 'yet/however/but' with replication concerns, the correct answer is the uncertainty option — never the original finding.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to grab the Friedmann finding before reading further and conclude pet owners are healthier.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 catches it: the very next sentence undermines the finding with 'mixed results'. The passage will not commit to 'less prone to illness' once it has flagged replication problems.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the word 'mutual' and assume the passage names a two-way benefit between pet and owner.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 traces every benefit clause — they all point toward the owner. The passage never reports what the pet gets out of the relationship in medical terms.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'overstating' and 'mixed results' is full debunking.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 also notes the 'Nevertheless, some people may gain psychological support' concession. Option D's 'wishful thinking' overshoots: the passage hedges, it does not dismiss.

host-2022-verb2-ELF-039.technique: When an ELF passage uses the 'positive finding → Yet/However → replication failure' structure, the right answer is always the calibrated uncertainty option. Look for words like 'unclear', 'mixed', 'may' in the option text.
host-2022-verb2-ELF-039.pitfall: The cure for the 'pick the original study' trap: always finish the paragraph before locking in. HP ELF passages frequently set up a finding only to qualify it within two sentences. The qualifier, not the finding, is the answer.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the passage names a study, then says 'efforts to replicate had mixed results', pick the option with 'unclear' or 'may' — not the study's headline finding.

host-2022-verb2-ELF-040.solution_path: The closing paragraphs say 'such projections are precisely what make this field ripe for psychology: they reveal our own social needs and desires' and 'animal relationships may someday provide useful comparison points to human connections — a benchmark for investigating empathy, caring and even decision making.' The research target is human relationships. Answer C.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need the MAJOR reason for current research — meaning the writer's framing of the field's main payoff, not a peripheral motive.

steps.title: Locate the field-purpose sentences
steps.text: Skip to the closing portion. The writer says projections from owners 'reveal our own social needs and desires' and that animal relationships will 'provide useful comparison points to human connections — a benchmark for investigating empathy, caring and even decision making'.

steps.title: Translate 'comparison points to human connections'
steps.text: A 'comparison point' is something you measure another thing against. 'Human connections' = relations between people. So animals are the benchmark; the target of study is close human relationships.

steps.title: Connect to the opening (full-circle check)
steps.text: Compare with the opening: 'a window into human sociality more broadly... how issues of identity, nurturing, support and attachment play out in a relationship'. Open and close agree: research illuminates HUMAN bonds. That repetition is the writer's main thesis.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.text: Option C says 'The prospect of new light being shed on close relations between people.' 'New light' = the research payoff; 'close relations between people' = the human connections being benchmarked. The option mirrors the closing sentences almost word for word.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer C. Insight in one line: when the opening and closing paragraphs of an ELF passage repeat the same direction of inquiry, that direction is the main-reason answer.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to remember the amygdala neurons and infer that researchers are chasing animal-human cognition links.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 fixes the actual research aim: human social patterns, not shared cognition. The neuron finding explained WHY we react to animals; it is not framed as the research goal.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at owner-pet bonds and assume behavioural similarity is the topic.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 catches it: 'comparison points' explicitly compares animal-bonds and human-bonds as parallel relationships — not as similar behaviour between owner and pet.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'pet research' is to assume the goal is understanding the ANIMALS.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 shows the full-circle structure: open and close both put humans as the target. Animals are the lens throughout; demystifying them is never named as the research motivation.

host-2022-verb2-ELF-040.technique: When an ELF passage's opening and closing paragraphs both point at the same target (e.g. human sociality), the major-reason answer must name that target. Look for echoes between the topic sentence and the final claim.
host-2022-verb2-ELF-040.pitfall: The cure for the 'wrong-direction' trap: write the research arrow on the page. If the passage says 'A is studied to learn about B', mark A → B and pick the option whose noun phrase matches B. Options that flip the arrow (B → A) lose every time.

pregrade_tactic.move: Compare the opening thesis to the closing claim — when they echo, the echo IS the answer; pick the option that names the shared target.

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-036.solution_path: The passage says four-year-olds who were 'paragons of executive function waited up to 15 minutes; school-age kids with high delay skills waited so long that Mischel himself could no longer stand to watch'. Older children outlasted younger children — a developmental difference in waiting ability. Answer A.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need what was SHOWN in the ORIGINAL marshmallow test — not later findings, not theories built on it.

steps.title: Find the original setup
steps.text: The 'so-called marshmallow test, first conducted at Stanford University in the late 1960s, monitors the ability of a child to resist a treat when left alone with it.' Subjects could ring a bell to eat one treat immediately or wait for two.

steps.title: Read the two age comparisons
steps.text: Four-year-olds at their best 'waited up to 15 minutes'. School-age kids (typically 5–11) with high skills 'waited so long that Mischel himself could no longer stand to watch'. School-age waited LONGER than four-year-olds. That is the developmental finding.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Older kids could wait longer than younger kids. Young children had MORE difficulty waiting for the bigger reward than more mature ones. That matches option A's wording exactly.

steps.title: Verify against the options
steps.text: Option A names the age effect; B names situational dependence (a LATER Mischel finding about personality, not the original test); C denies age effects; D introduces adults who weren't tested. Only A fits the original test as described.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer A. Insight in one line: when an ELF question asks what an ORIGINAL study showed, restrict yourself to data from the experiment itself — not theories the researcher developed later.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read Mischel's later situationalist views back into the original test.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 locates the original test in the 1960s; step 3 reads its findings. Situationalism comes later in the passage as a separate research thread. The original test demonstrated age differences, not situational determination.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'paragons of executive function' and infer age was secondary to individual skill.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 directly contradicts this: the passage compares four-year-olds to school-age kids and reports different waiting times. Age was clearly relevant — older could wait longer.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on willpower research is to expect a children-versus-adults comparison.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 5 catches it: the original test used children only. No adult control group is mentioned. The option imports a comparison that the passage never makes.

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-036.technique: When the question specifies 'original' or 'first', restrict to what that experiment alone demonstrated. Strip out later theoretical extensions before scanning options.
host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-036.pitfall: The cure for the 'later-theory' trap: highlight 'original' in the prompt and treat any option mentioning concepts the passage adds later (situational triggers, if-then patterns) as automatically out of scope for this question.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the prompt says 'original' or 'first', restrict to data from the experiment itself — discard any option that imports later theoretical layers.

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-037.solution_path: The passage says the 'cooling mental transformations that help a kid wait... are the BEDROCK PROCESSES that allow that same person to stay future-focused enough to earn a college degree or to reliably invest in a long-term pension plan'. The same underlying mechanism drives both childhood waiting and adult planning — that connection is what makes the test broadly interesting. Answer C.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need why the test has WIDER psychological interest — beyond the immediate childhood finding. The keyword is 'wider'.

steps.title: Find the bridge sentence
steps.text: Locate: 'The cooling mental transformations that help a kid wait agonizing minutes... are the bedrock processes that allow that same person to stay future-focused enough to earn a college degree or to reliably invest in a long-term pension plan.'

steps.title: Translate 'bedrock processes'
steps.text: 'Bedrock' = foundational, underlying. 'Same person' connects child and adult. The sentence says one underlying mechanism explains both the toddler resisting a marshmallow and the adult investing in a pension. Same machinery, different outputs.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.text: Option C: 'the same kind of underlying reasoning explains a person's behavior regardless of age'. 'Underlying reasoning' = bedrock processes; 'regardless of age' = same person from child to adult. Direct mapping.

steps.title: Verify by checking the prefrontal cortex line
steps.text: Backing this: 'Those with the ability to delay have a well-oiled prefrontal cortex that successfully regulates their abstract thinking as well as impulse control.' Same brain region, dual function — child impulse control AND adult abstract thinking. Confirms the unified-mechanism reading.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer C. Insight in one line: 'wider interest' questions reward options that show a single mechanism explaining many behaviours across the lifespan — not options that report a specific finding.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'same person' as 'same results' and infer adults perform identically to children.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 separates the two: the MECHANISM is shared, not the behaviour. The passage compares marshmallow-waiting to pension-investing — clearly not identical behaviours, just outputs of the same underlying control system.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'prefrontal cortex' and infer the wider relevance is about brain dysfunction.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 5 reads the brain line in context: the prefrontal cortex is named as the source of SUCCESS (delay, future-focus), not dysfunction. The ADHD mention is about cultural interest, not the test's psychological payoff.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on waiting tasks is stress tolerance.
distractors.why_wrong: Stress tolerance is named as one correlate, not THE reason for wider interest. Step 2 locates the actual answer in the bedrock-processes sentence, which goes much broader — academic success, income, life outcomes — than just stress.

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-037.technique: When an ELF passage links childhood and adult outcomes through one mechanism (bedrock processes, prefrontal cortex, if-then patterns), the wider-interest answer names that shared mechanism, not any specific outcome.
host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-037.pitfall: The cure for the 'pick a single correlate' trap: 'wider interest' or 'broader implication' phrasing in the prompt is a signal — the right option will be the most ABSTRACT framing in the list, not the most concrete finding.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the passage uses 'bedrock', 'underlying', 'same person', the right answer names the SHARED mechanism behind disparate behaviours — pick the most abstract option, not a specific correlate.

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-038.solution_path: Tolstoy 'preached a love of humanity' yet 'fought incessantly with his wife' and 'was critical and demanding of his family'. He embodied a gap between professed ideals and actual behaviour — and Mischel uses him to illustrate that personality manifests differently in different situations. Answer B.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need the rhetorical purpose Tolstoy serves in this article — why the writer chose this specific example.

steps.title: Catalogue what the passage says about Tolstoy
steps.text: (i) 'A man of ferocious complexity and contradiction'. (ii) 'Fought incessantly with his wife.' (iii) 'Critical and demanding of his family even as he preached a love of humanity.' Every fact pairs a stated ideal with a contrary behaviour.

steps.title: Read the framing sentence
steps.text: Right after the Tolstoy list, the writer says 'This may surprise some people, but it would not surprise Mischel.' Mischel's explanation follows — the if-then theory says personality looks contradictory because behaviour depends on situation.

steps.title: Name the rhetorical role
steps.text: Tolstoy is exhibit A for a person whose stated ideals (love humanity) clash with day-to-day behaviour (cruel to family). That is the textbook 'ideals vs actual behaviour' gap.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.text: Option B: 'an illustration of the conflict between personal ideals and actual behavior.' Direct match for the preach-love/fight-family contradiction.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer B. Insight in one line: when an ELF passage uses a named historical figure, ask 'what specific thing about this person illustrates the theory?' — the answer is usually a stated-vs-actual gap or a context-dependent behaviour.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to lean on Tolstoy's fame as a writer and assume he stands for writers in general.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 catalogues the actual claims about Tolstoy — none of them concern his writing or generalise to other writers. He is used as a personality case, not a writer-type.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the contradictions and conclude that self-control as a concept must be unstable.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 catches the opposite: Mischel uses Tolstoy to STRENGTHEN his if-then theory, not undermine self-control. The passage explicitly frames Tolstoy's contradictions as PREDICTABLE under Mischel's framework.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a 'troubled' biography is to suggest therapy.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 names the role: theoretical illustration, not clinical case. The passage proposes no treatment for Tolstoy — only an explanation of him.

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-038.technique: When an ELF passage drops a famous biographical example next to a theoretical claim, the question is almost always 'what does this person show'. Look for the specific pairing (stated X but did Y) that the passage emphasises.
host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-038.pitfall: The cure for 'use famous trait of the person' trap: ignore everything you know ABOUT the historical figure outside the passage. Only the specific facts the writer chose to include count for the question.

pregrade_tactic.move: When a famous example is introduced, list what they SAID and what they DID inside the passage — the mismatch between those two is the illustration the writer wants you to name.

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-039.solution_path: Mischel proposes that 'personality inscribes itself in if-then behavioral patterns' — meaning 'the behavioral signature of personality specifies what the individual does predictably if particular situational triggers occur.' Personality produces a given behaviour only when a matching trigger appears. Answer C.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need the BASIC POINT of the if-then argument — the most compact possible summary of Mischel's claim.

steps.title: Locate the if-then sentence
steps.text: 'He argues that the key to exercising self-control and to understanding why people appear to behave erratically resides in the slimmest of phrases: if-then.' Followed by the job/home conscientiousness example.

steps.title: Unpack the job/home example
steps.text: Being conscientious at work doesn't predict being conscientious about bills at home. BUT it does predict you'll keep being conscientious AT WORK. So personality is consistent WITHIN a context. The 'if' is the situation; the 'then' is the behaviour.

steps.title: Read the formal claim
steps.text: 'The behavioral signature of personality specifies what the individual does predictably IF particular situational triggers occur.' Translation: personality + matching situation = predictable behaviour. No situation match = no expression.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.text: Option C: 'Personality may give rise to a certain behavior only under specific conditions.' 'Specific conditions' = situational triggers; 'give rise to behavior' = the 'then' output. Tight match.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer C. Insight in one line: 'if-then' in psychology means 'conditional on context' — pick the option that frames behaviour as CONDITIONAL on specific situations.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'erratically' in the passage as 'no link between personality and action'.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 establishes the opposite: Mischel says behaviour is PREDICTABLE — just within a situation rather than across all situations. The link exists; it is conditional, not absent.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'patterns... characterize most people' and infer that behavioural norms are doing the work.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 names the source: each person has their OWN if-then pattern. The passage is not about shared norms — it is about person-specific situational triggers.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on contradictory behaviour is to declare it inexplicable.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 catches it: Mischel's whole point is that if-then patterns RESOLVE the apparent mystery. Option D's 'neither personality nor behavior can explain' is the exact position the passage refutes.

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-039.technique: When an ELF passage names an 'if-then' or 'situational trigger' theory, the right answer always frames behaviour as conditional, not absent or random. Look for option words like 'only under', 'when', 'in certain'.
host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-039.pitfall: The cure for 'no link / unpredictable' traps: 'if-then' explicitly preserves predictability — the trick is that the IF must match. Reject any option claiming behaviour is unexplainable or unlinked.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the passage names an 'if-then' or 'situational trigger' theory, pick the option with 'only under', 'when', or 'specific conditions' — never the one calling behaviour random or unlinked.

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-040.solution_path: The closing paragraph ends 'Mischel has said that he is most interested in the subjects who failed the marshmallow test but went on to succeed in life.' Mischel highlights the OUTLIERS — the cases that don't follow the standard prediction. The test, on this reading, also exposes less-typical personality profiles for study. Answer D.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need a conclusion that follows from the CLOSING paragraph specifically. Not the whole article; the final framing of the test.

steps.title: Read the closing sentences carefully
steps.text: Two key claims: 'We are, each of us, that complicated' and 'Mischel has said that he is most interested in the subjects who failed the marshmallow test but went on to succeed in life. There's no way to know whether Tolstoy would fit the bill, but if so, Mischel would have an idea why.'

steps.title: Identify the focal group
steps.text: 'Failed the test but succeeded in life' = atypical cases. They contradict the simple prediction (low delay skill → poor outcomes). Mischel says these are the MOST interesting subjects — the test's value extends to studying them.

steps.title: Translate to plain English
steps.text: The marshmallow test isn't just a screening tool that sorts winners from losers. It also flags atypical individuals — people whose later life doesn't match their childhood result — and that makes it useful for studying unusual personality profiles.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.text: Option D: 'It gives an opportunity to study some less typical personality profiles.' 'Less typical' = the failure-then-success group; 'opportunity to study' = Mischel's research interest. Match.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer D. Insight in one line: when an ELF passage's final sentence names a researcher's 'most interested in' group, that group identifies the conclusion the writer wants you to draw.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'continuing interest' from earlier paragraphs as a promise of improvement.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 stays inside the closing paragraph: nothing there mentions improving the test. The 'most interested in failures who succeed' line points to a research direction, not a methodological upgrade.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at Mischel's interest in 'failures' and infer that the test is methodologically suspect.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 reads it the other way: Mischel still treats the test as central — the failure-then-success cases are intellectually exciting, not evidence the test is broken.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a famous test is to praise its predictive power.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 catches the contradiction: the closing paragraph specifically highlights the cases where prediction FAILED. That is the opposite of 'even more remarkable prediction'.

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-040.technique: When an ELF passage closes by naming a researcher's preferred subgroup, look for the option that frames the test or theory as useful for studying THAT subgroup — usually the atypical or outlier cases.
host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-040.pitfall: The cure for the 'praise the test' trap: scan the closing paragraph for any negative or counter-example framing ('failed', 'unexpected', 'surprising'). When the writer ends on a counter-example, the answer is about studying counter-examples, not celebrating the rule.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the closing paragraph names 'most interested in those who failed/succeeded against type', pick the option that frames the test as a tool for studying atypical profiles.

var-2024-verb1-ELF-031.solution_path: The passage debunks the farmer myth and credits 'industrialists and politicians' for lobbying for DST. Energy savings are 'mixed'. But the passage commits to one lasting consequence: DST 'succeeded in another one of its initial goals: getting more people to shop. That trend continues for many retailers today.' More daylight → more shopping → commercial profits. Answer D.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: 'Implied' means a conclusion the passage strongly supports without stating directly. Scan for what the passage commits to as fact.

steps.title: Catalogue the certified claims
steps.text: (i) Farmer-myth: actually lobbied against DST. (ii) Industrialists and Woodrow Wilson pushed for it. (iii) Energy data 'remains mixed'. (iv) Shopping increase: 'has succeeded... That trend continues for many retailers today.'

steps.title: Find the only confirmed consequence
steps.text: Energy: uncertain. Shopping: confirmed and ongoing. So the only fully endorsed lasting effect is the retail/commercial one. That is the implication.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.text: Option D: 'More daylight hours had a positive effect on commercial profits.' Maps to 'getting more people to shop... continues for many retailers'.

steps.title: Verify by elimination
steps.text: A claims farmer reluctance (text says active opposition, not reluctance). B claims unforeseen consequences (energy data is unclear, but the shopping result was the STATED goal — not unforeseen). C claims energy-lobby support (Wilson wanted to DECREASE electricity demand — energy industry opposite of beneficiary).

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer D. Insight in one line: in short ELF passages, 'implied' usually maps to the one claim the writer hedges least about — find the confirmed-and-lasting consequence.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'farm lobby did participate' and infer hesitation or reluctance.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 catches it: farmers 'were actually lobbying AGAINST DST' — that is an active stance, not a reluctance to take one.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'mixed data on energy' and call the energy effect an unforeseen consequence.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 5 separates the categories: mixed-data = uncertainty about the planned effect, not an unforeseen new effect. The shopping outcome was actually one of DST's 'initial goals', so it was foreseen.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on energy and politicians is to assume the energy industry pushed DST.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 records Wilson's reason: DST would DECREASE electricity demand. The energy industry would lose revenue from that — they have no incentive to lobby for it. The push came from industrialists and politicians, not the energy lobby.

var-2024-verb1-ELF-031.technique: For 'implied' questions on short paragraphs, list every certified claim and find the one that maps cleanly to an option. Hedged claims ('mixed', 'unclear') are usually traps; confirmed claims ('succeeded', 'continues today') are usually the answer.
var-2024-verb1-ELF-031.pitfall: The cure for the 'misread the lobbyist' trap: track who BENEFITS from a policy and who LOSES. The DST-energy logic flips the usual energy-industry direction — Wilson wanted to cut their revenue, not boost it.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the passage hedges most claims but commits to one ('has succeeded', 'continues today'), pick the option that names that confirmed consequence.

var-2024-verb1-ELF-032.solution_path: Previously, contagious yawning was known in 'humans, dogs, chimps and a species of rodent' — all mammals. The new study shows budgies, a bird, also yawn contagiously, and the writer notes 'social non-mammals may have basic forms of empathy.' The novelty is adding a non-mammal to the empathy-linked group. Answer B.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need what is SPECIFICALLY NEW — not just any finding, but the contrast between this study and the prior state of knowledge.

steps.title: Catalogue the prior knowledge
steps.text: 'Many vertebrates yawn — but only humans, dogs, chimps and a species of rodent find yawns contagious.' All four prior species are mammals. That is the baseline.

steps.title: Read the new finding
steps.text: 'Now we can add budgies to the list.' Budgies are birds — non-mammals. Same yawning contagion behaviour. Plus: 'social non-mammals may have basic forms of empathy.'

steps.title: Name the novelty
steps.text: What is genuinely new: empathy-linked contagion is no longer mammal-only. A bird shows it too. So empathy may not be exclusive to mammal species.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.text: Option B: 'Apparently, showing signs of empathy is not exclusive to mammal species.' 'Apparently' hedges (matches the passage's 'may'); 'not exclusive to mammals' captures the new addition of a bird.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer B. Insight in one line: 'specifically new' questions usually reward the option that captures the contrast between the prior list and the new entry — find the category that just expanded.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to grab the '3x as likely to yawn within five minutes' detail and read it as 'catches on faster than thought'.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 catches it: the 3x figure measures the strength of the contagion in budgies — not a revised global rate. The NEW thing is the species, not the speed.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'empathetic processes' and infer empathy is everywhere.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 lists only five species across both mammals and now one bird — that is a narrow group, not 'most animals'. Option C drastically over-extends the claim.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on budgies (a popular pet) is pet-specific imitation.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 includes chimps — wild, not kept as pets — in the contagious-yawn group. Pet status isn't the dividing line; the line is mammal/non-mammal, which step 5 names.

var-2024-verb1-ELF-032.technique: When an ELF short text reports a new finding extending a list, pinpoint the CATEGORY the list just crossed. If the prior list was mammals-only and a bird joined, the novelty is non-mammalian membership.
var-2024-verb1-ELF-032.pitfall: The cure for the 'wrong-axis novelty' trap: separate WHAT was found from WHY it matters. The percentage detail (3x) is incidental; the species expansion is the conceptual news.

pregrade_tactic.move: When a study adds one new member to a pre-existing list, identify the category boundary it crosses — that boundary is the novelty the answer must name.

var-2024-verb1-ELF-033.solution_path: The first paragraph says historians value old jokes because 'if you can get the jokes of the past, you can understand the interests and sensibilities of the people who inhabited it'. Humour is treated as a doorway into past mentalities — a way of reading historical reasoning. Answer D.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need the main point about the STUDY of humour — the writer's framing of why historians look at jokes — in the FIRST paragraph only.

steps.title: Locate the methodological claim
steps.text: The opening notes historians value jokes; cultural history of the 1970s started this work; and 'it has become a well-trodden path to explore past mentalities.' That's the method statement.

steps.title: Read the theory sentence
steps.text: 'The theory is simple: if you can get the jokes of the past, you can understand the interests and sensibilities of the people who inhabited it.' Jokes = key; past minds = lock. Humour as historical lens.

steps.title: Translate to plain English
steps.text: Studying humour gives access to how past people thought. It is a gateway into their reasoning, their tastes, their anxieties.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.text: Option D: 'There are arguments that humour provides a gateway to the reasoning of former times.' 'Gateway to reasoning of former times' = 'understand the interests and sensibilities of the people who inhabited' the past. Direct mapping.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer D. Insight in one line: when a passage's first paragraph frames its topic as a TOOL for studying something else, the answer names the topic-as-tool relationship.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'Aristotle... separates us from animals' as a uniqueness claim about humour.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 traces the paragraph's actual point: humour is a HISTORICAL TOOL. The Aristotle line is a passing reference, not the main argument. The paragraph doesn't claim 'uniquely human' as a new finding.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at Aristotle's mention and infer the paragraph debunks his view.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 catches it: the paragraph mentions Aristotle once, without disagreement. It does not 'basically refute' his view — it leverages a historicist insight instead.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on humour-across-time is to claim similar targets everywhere.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 contradicts this: the passage explicitly says 'the subjects we see fit to laugh at change over time'. Targets are NOT remarkably similar across regions and history — the variability is what makes them useful evidence.

var-2024-verb1-ELF-033.technique: When an ELF passage's first paragraph asks 'why do historians study X?', the main-point answer always names X-as-evidence — humour as a window onto past minds, jokes as a key to historical sensibilities.
var-2024-verb1-ELF-033.pitfall: The cure for 'pick the named-philosopher' trap: a single name (Aristotle, Hobbes) dropped in the first paragraph is usually scenery, not the main argument. Strip the name and re-read the thesis sentence.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the paragraph frames a topic as a way to access past minds, pick the option naming 'gateway', 'window', or 'tool to understand' — never the option that treats the topic as the END.

var-2024-verb1-ELF-034.solution_path: Corfield's 1997 article 'Laughing at the Learned' argued that jokes about lawyers, doctors and clergymen 'were a means both to voice discontent and to fight back. By serving up a barrage of hostile wit, ordinary people could exercise informal moral controls.' Jokes were a weapon against the dubious power of the learned professions. Answer A.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need CORFIELD'S conclusion specifically about 18th- and 19th-century jokes — not the wider article's view, not later researchers'.

steps.title: Locate Corfield's argument
steps.text: Corfield 'exposed the degree of anxiety felt at the increasing power of the learned professions. They occupied a privileged position... and were thus well placed to take the laity for a ride.' Jokes, she argued, 'were a means both to voice discontent and to fight back.'

steps.title: Identify the targets and function
steps.text: Targets: lawyers, medical experts, clergymen — the 'learned professions' wielding suspect power. Function: 'hostile wit' as 'informal moral controls'. Jokes as resistance to elite authority.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Ordinary people used jokes to push back against powerful experts whose attitudes they suspected. The dubious attitude of the powerful was the target; humour was the tool of opposition.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.text: Option A: 'They were used as a way to oppose the dubious attitudes of people in power.' 'Oppose' = fight back; 'dubious attitudes of people in power' = take-the-laity-for-a-ride learned professions. Tight match.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer A. Insight in one line: when an ELF passage names a researcher's specific argument with verbs like 'fight back' or 'voice discontent', pick the option that names opposition.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'jestbooks' and assume jokes were primarily escapist entertainment.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 catches it: Corfield's verbs are 'voice discontent' and 'fight back' — active resistance, not passive distraction. Option B converts active opposition into mere coping.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'clergymen' as a target and infer church censorship was the dynamic.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 lists the targets — the clergy were targets OF jokes, not censors of them. Corfield's argument runs from ordinary people TOWARD the powerful, not the other way.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'informal moral controls' is that elites imposed them top-down.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 reverses the direction: ordinary people exercised the moral controls via jokes against elites. Option D inverts the power flow Corfield described.

var-2024-verb1-ELF-034.technique: When a passage attributes an argument to a named researcher and uses verbs of resistance ('fight back', 'voice discontent', 'hostile wit'), the answer captures bottom-up opposition. Verify the direction of the action.
var-2024-verb1-ELF-034.pitfall: The cure for the 'reverse-direction' trap: draw an arrow from joke-tellers to joke-targets. Corfield's arrow points from common people to elites — any option that reverses the arrow (elite morals onto commoners) loses.

pregrade_tactic.move: When a researcher names jokes as 'fighting back' or 'hostile wit', draw the arrow from joke-teller to target; pick the option whose direction matches that arrow.

var-2024-verb1-ELF-035.solution_path: The passage lists 18th-century joke targets: 'lawyers, medical experts and clergymen', 'politicians', 'elites and their fashions', 'strutting fops', and 'social superiors'. The unifying trait is high prestige — these were all people occupying privileged positions in society. Answer C.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need what is SAID about the targets of jokes in 18th-century Britain — meaning the writer's characterisation of who was being mocked.

steps.title: List every target named
steps.text: (i) Lawyers, medical experts, clergymen (the 'learned professions'). (ii) Politicians ('weathercock'). (iii) Elites and their fashions (perfumed fops). (iv) 'Social superiors' outwitted by plucky underdogs.

steps.title: Find the common trait
steps.text: Every group shares one feature: high social standing. Privileged knowledge, political power, elite fashion, social superiority. The category is PRESTIGE, not any specific profession.

steps.title: Read the writer's own summary
steps.text: The passage uses the phrase 'jokes at the expense of the powerful' and 'laughing at the powerful was not just a pointed weapon'. The writer explicitly groups all targets under 'the powerful' / high prestige.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.text: Option C: 'They were basically people of high prestige in society.' 'High prestige' captures the unifying trait across the four listed groups.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer C. Insight in one line: when an ELF passage lists multiple targets across professions, the answer names the COMMON TRAIT they share — usually power, prestige, or status.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to focus on 'fops' and 'social superiors' and conclude the targets were the nobility.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 lists lawyers, doctors, clergy and politicians — none of them are royalty or nobility in the strict sense. The category is broader: high status across multiple fields, not aristocratic birth.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'as ever' next to politicians and infer that the targets matched other historical periods.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 identifies the category. The 'as ever' phrase only notes politicians get mocked across eras — it doesn't say the SAME PROFESSIONS were targeted in other periods. Option B over-generalises.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on multiple target types is to call it a dramatic shift.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 catches it: the passage groups ALL targets under 'the powerful' — that is one stable category, not a shift between two. No contrast between earlier-learned-only and later-political target groups appears.

var-2024-verb1-ELF-035.technique: When an ELF passage lists multiple targets across professions, find the abstract trait that unifies them. The correct option will name that trait at the level of CLASS (powerful, prestigious, elite) rather than any specific profession.
var-2024-verb1-ELF-035.pitfall: The cure for the 'pick one listed group' trap: the right answer is almost never one of the listed professions — it is the umbrella category the writer placed them under. Look for 'the powerful', 'elites', 'social superiors' as keywords.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the passage lists multiple specific targets, find the umbrella term the writer uses ('the powerful', 'social superiors') — pick the option that matches that umbrella, not any single listed profession.

var-2024-verb1-ELF-036.solution_path: The passage names a key 18th-century theory: 'we laugh when we perceive ill-suited pairings of ideas, images or situations.' Francis Hutcheson made this concrete — 'something comical in juxtaposing an object, person or event of great seriousness with one that was not', e.g. 'any little accident befalling a person of great gravity'. Humour arises from clever contrast between mismatched elements. Answer A.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need a main argument made BY 18TH-CENTURY scholars (not by modern researchers) about the BASIS of humour — what makes things funny.

steps.title: Locate the theory sentence
steps.text: 'One of the key theories was that we laugh when we perceive ill-suited pairings of ideas, images or situations.' That is the headline 18th-century claim about humour's basis.

steps.title: Unpack with Hutcheson's example
steps.text: Francis Hutcheson, the named Scottish philosopher, said 'there was something comical in juxtaposing an object, person or event of great seriousness with one that was not'. Examples: a dignified person tripping ('any little accident befalling a person of great gravity'), 'the dirtying of a decent dress'. Mismatch of dignity and triviality.

steps.title: Translate to plain English
steps.text: Funny things happen when two unlikely partners are forced together — a serious person and a banana peel, a clean dress and a mud puddle. The HUMOUR is in the unexpected contrast.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.text: Option A: 'Humour stems from clever use of unexpected contrasts.' 'Unexpected contrast' = ill-suited pairing / juxtaposition of serious with not-serious. Direct match.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer A. Insight in one line: when an ELF passage names a theory using words like 'juxtapose', 'pairing', or 'mismatch', the answer captures CONTRAST as the source of the effect.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'any little accident befalling a person' as 'real events make the best jokes'.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 reads Hutcheson's example as a PRINCIPLE, not a journalism standard. He uses 'any little accident' to mean 'in any situation where you can manufacture this contrast', not as a requirement that the events be authentic.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'accident' and infer the basis is improbability or coincidence.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 separates the two: the contrast is in DIGNITY versus TRIVIALITY, not in how likely the event is. A dignified person tripping is funny because of the mismatch, not because tripping is rare.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on philosophers theorising humour is to assume they equate humour with philosophy.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 5 fixes the claim's content: the theory describes humour's MECHANISM (contrast). It does not say humour resembles philosophical reasoning in any way.

var-2024-verb1-ELF-036.technique: When an ELF passage names a theory of humour using vocabulary like 'juxtapose', 'pairing', 'ill-suited', 'mismatch' — the right answer reformulates this as CONTRAST or CLASH between unlikely partners.
var-2024-verb1-ELF-036.pitfall: The cure for 'pick a concrete example' trap: Hutcheson's accidents and dirty dresses are ILLUSTRATIONS of the principle, not the principle itself. Pull back to the abstract mechanism before matching options.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the passage names a theory with 'ill-suited pairings' or 'juxtaposing serious with not-serious', pick the option that names CONTRAST or MISMATCH as the source of humour.

var-2024-verb1-ELF-037.solution_path: The passage concludes about 18th-century humour: 'Laughing at the powerful was not just a pointed weapon; it was also supremely enjoyable.' Two coexisting functions — opposition (weapon) and entertainment (enjoyable). Humour did both at once. Answer C.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need a CONCLUSION specifically about 18th-century humour — the writer's overall verdict, usually placed near the end of the discussion of that century.

steps.title: Locate the conclusion sentence
steps.text: 'Laughing at the powerful was not just a pointed weapon; it was also supremely enjoyable.' The structure 'not just X; it was also Y' is a dual-function claim — both X and Y simultaneously.

steps.title: Name the two functions
steps.text: Function 1: 'pointed weapon' = a tool of opposition against the powerful (echoes Corfield's 'hostile wit'). Function 2: 'supremely enjoyable' = pure entertainment, pleasure, 'intellectual tickling'. The conclusion links both.

steps.title: Translate to plain English
steps.text: 18th-century humour wasn't only about resistance to elites; it was also genuinely entertaining. Two purposes coexisted in the same jokes.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.text: Option C: 'It was associated with opposition as well as pure entertainment.' 'Opposition' = pointed weapon; 'pure entertainment' = supremely enjoyable. Direct mapping.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer C. Insight in one line: when an ELF passage uses 'not just X; also Y' near a topic's conclusion, the answer combines both X and Y — never picks just one.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the 1741 newspaper reference and infer the press homogenised humour.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 keeps the conclusion focused: the passage uses the 1741 article as evidence of laughter's pleasure, not as a force unifying jokes. Option A imports a media effect the passage never claims.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'politicians' and 'powerful' and assume political humour was uniquely intense.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 catches it: the passage says politicians were targets 'as ever' — political humour wasn't a 18th-century specialty. Option B's 'rarely seen in other historical periods' isn't supported.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a flourishing humour discussion is to infer the era had unusually much humour.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 reads the passage's actual claim: laughter received unusual SCRUTINY between the 16th and 18th centuries, but the passage never compares humour volume across periods. Option D over-extends.

var-2024-verb1-ELF-037.technique: When an ELF passage's conclusion uses 'not just X; it was also Y', look for the option that names BOTH X and Y. Single-function options are traps; the answer captures the duality.
var-2024-verb1-ELF-037.pitfall: The cure for the 'pick one function' trap: highlight the 'not just / also' construction. Whenever it appears, the answer must combine both halves — never settle for an option that names only one.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the passage uses 'not just X; also Y', the answer must name both X and Y — reject any option that captures only one half.

var-2024-verb1-ELF-038.solution_path: The mouse study found a two-stage process: 'during the first three weeks, bones grew STRONGER as the fluoxetine impaired osteoclasts', but 'by six weeks, the higher levels of serotonin prompted by the drug DISRUPTED the ability of the hypothalamus region of the brain to promote bone growth.' Short term: positive. Long term: negative. Ducy says the same two-phase pattern is seen in people. Long-term effects are completely different from short-term effects. Answer A.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need a conclusion about the link between depression, antidepressants and bone fractures based on the mouse study and its human relevance.

steps.title: Read the two phases
steps.text: Phase 1 (first three weeks): 'bones grew STRONGER' because fluoxetine impaired the cells that normally deplete bone. Phase 2 (by six weeks): higher serotonin 'DISRUPTED' the brain's ability to promote bone growth — bones now lose mass.

steps.title: Name the contrast
steps.text: Short term: drug helps bones. Long term: drug harms bones. The two effects are OPPOSITE — not just different in degree but in direction. That is the headline finding.

steps.title: Check the human transfer
steps.text: 'Ducy says this two-phase pattern is also seen in people.' So the same opposite-effects timeline applies to humans, not just mice.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.text: Option A: 'Long-term effects of antidepressants are completely different from short-term effects.' 'Completely different' = opposite direction (strengthen → weaken). Matches the two-phase pattern.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer A. Insight in one line: when an ELF passage describes a two-phase study with phases pointing OPPOSITE directions, the answer names the short-vs-long-term contrast.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to be sceptical of mouse data and conclude it doesn't transfer to humans.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 catches it: the passage explicitly says 'this two-phase pattern is also seen in people'. The mouse data isn't being undermined as evidence for humans — it is being validated by human observation.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the question's mention of depression and assume the conclusion is about depression's direct effects.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 reads the study: the experiment tested FLUOXETINE (the drug), not untreated depression. The passage explicitly says it wasn't previously clear if fractures came from drug or depression — and now points to the drug.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on bone problems is to assume both depression and drugs hurt bones similarly.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 catches it: the study describes one substance (the drug) producing opposite effects across time — not a comparison between depression and antidepressants. Option D imports a comparison the passage doesn't make.

var-2024-verb1-ELF-038.technique: When an ELF short text reports a two-phase pharmacological study with opposite effects, the answer names the SHORT-VS-LONG contrast. Look for option words 'short term', 'long term', 'two phases', 'different over time'.
var-2024-verb1-ELF-038.pitfall: The cure for the 'mouse-vs-human' trap: always check whether the passage extends mouse findings to humans. If the line 'also seen in people' appears, treat the mouse data as valid for human conclusions.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the passage names a short-term effect and a long-term effect going opposite directions, pick the option that names the time-dependent reversal — not the option doubting the species transfer.

var-2024-verb1-ELF-039.solution_path: The passage describes Nevada as 'a laboratory for how Americans define freedom' and recounts the 1860 Pyramid Lake War where settlers were 'exerting their freedom by usurping Paiute grazing land and disrupting food supplies'. The ironic use of 'freedom' to describe land seizure is the writer's signal that Nevada exemplifies the larger American colonisation pattern. Answer D.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: 'Implied' = strongly suggested but not stated outright. You need the writer's larger point about US colonisation, conveyed by the Nevada example.

steps.title: Catch the framing phrase
steps.text: 'Nevada has been described as a laboratory for how Americans define freedom.' 'Laboratory' = a representative case, a model — Nevada stands in for the larger American freedom project, not as an outlier.

steps.title: Read the ironic 'freedom' usage
steps.text: Settlers 'were exerting their freedom by usurping Paiute grazing land and disrupting food supplies'. The word 'freedom' in scare-quote register describes seizing other people's land and starving them out. The irony tells you the writer sees this as the typical, not the exception.

steps.title: Connect the laboratory and the irony
steps.text: Nevada is the LAB; settler 'freedom' = land seizure; the natives' loss is the price. Therefore Nevada illustrates the standard pattern of 19th-century US conquest — settlers expanding under the banner of freedom, at indigenous expense.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.text: Option D: 'It was typical of how America was conquered in the 19th century.' 'Typical' = the laboratory / model status; matches the writer's framing that Nevada exemplifies a wider pattern.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer D. Insight in one line: when an ELF short text frames a place as 'a laboratory for X', the implication is that the place EXEMPLIFIES the larger X pattern, not departs from it.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the brisk tone as downplaying violence, suggesting Nevada was milder.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 catches it: 'usurping' and 'disrupting food supplies' are explicitly violent descriptions. The passage describes seizure and starvation — nothing 'less brutal' about that.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the four named native peoples and infer fragmentation among them.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 reads the actual claim: the passage refers to 'allied native forces' — the tribes coordinated against settlers. There is no internal-conflict claim in the text.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a war between native and settler forces is to imagine native tribes on both sides.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 fixes the alignment: natives on one side, 'US Army-backed settlers' on the other. No native tribe is described as siding with the settlers in this conflict.

var-2024-verb1-ELF-039.technique: When an ELF passage frames a specific case as 'a laboratory for' or 'a microcosm of' something larger, the implied conclusion is that the case represents the typical pattern — not an exception. Watch for the laboratory/typical signal.
var-2024-verb1-ELF-039.pitfall: The cure for the 'subtle outlier' trap: 'laboratory' and ironic scare-quote 'freedom' are signals that the case is exemplary, not unusual. Reject options claiming the case was atypical, milder, or different from the norm.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the passage calls a place 'a laboratory for' a larger pattern, pick the option that says 'typical of' or 'representative of' that pattern — never the option claiming it was an exception.

var-2024-verb2-ELF-036.solution_path: The first two paragraphs report Snow's complaint: literary intellectuals 'were shamefully unembarrassed about not grasping, say, the second law of thermodynamics — even though asking if someone knows it is about the scientific equivalent of: Have you read a work of Shakespeare's?' His lament is that the literary side lacks basic scientific knowledge while pretending the gap doesn't matter. Answer A.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need SNOW'S complaint — his specific grievance about the two-cultures split — using only the FIRST TWO paragraphs.

steps.title: Locate Snow's specific accusation
steps.text: 'Snow largely blamed literary types for this gulf of mutual incomprehension. These intellectuals, Snow asserted, were shamefully unembarrassed about not grasping, say, the second law of thermodynamics.'

steps.title: Read the symmetry argument
steps.text: Snow's analogy: not knowing the second law of thermodynamics is, scientifically, the equivalent of never having read Shakespeare. Translation — a basic scientific concept should be cultural common knowledge, and literary intellectuals scandalously lack it.

steps.title: Name the complaint
steps.text: Snow is complaining about LITERARY intellectuals' lack of basic SCIENTIFIC knowledge. The target of the complaint is literary people; the content is their scientific illiteracy.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.text: Option A: 'He worried about the lack of basic scientific knowledge among literary people.' 'Basic scientific knowledge' = second law of thermodynamics; 'literary people' = literary intellectuals. Direct match.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer A. Insight in one line: when the passage names Snow's complaint with a specific example (thermodynamics ↔ Shakespeare), the answer captures that target — scientific illiteracy among literary people.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to misread 'two cultures' as a critique of science's cultural dominance.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 catches it: Snow CHAMPIONED science (an English physicist himself) and complained about literary types failing to grasp it. He did not argue that science had too much attention.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'gulf of mutual incomprehension' and infer scientists were the ones being patronising.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 traces the direction of blame: Snow blames LITERARY intellectuals for the gulf, not scientists. He doesn't describe scientists as patronising — he describes literary types as scientifically illiterate.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a 'two cultures' essay is equal-value balance argument.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 5 catches it: Snow argues a SPECIFIC ASYMMETRY — literary people lack basic scientific knowledge. The option's 'considered of equal value' frames the position as parity, but Snow was actually demanding literary people upgrade their science.

var-2024-verb2-ELF-036.technique: When an ELF passage attributes a specific complaint to a named figure backed by an analogy (X is to Y as A is to B), the right answer reproduces the analogy's content — here, scientific basics matter as much as literary basics.
var-2024-verb2-ELF-036.pitfall: The cure for the 'reverse the blame' trap: Snow blames literary types, not scientists. Track who is being accused; reject options that flip the accuser and accused.

pregrade_tactic.move: When Snow asserts a parallel (thermodynamics is to scientific literacy as Shakespeare is to literary literacy), pick the option that names the same asymmetric complaint — literary side lacking the science basics.

var-2024-verb2-ELF-037.solution_path: The writer is critical of Snow's cultural typologies ('not exactly subtle', stereotyped) but says 'Snow's expression of this optimism is dated, yet his thoughts about progress are more relevant today than his cultural typologies' and that the science-direction question 'is the aspect of Snow's book that speaks most directly to us today'. Snow's views on the future still have value. Answer C.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: You need a statement most in keeping with the WRITER's views on Snow's ideas — the article's overall stance, mixing critique with what holds up.

steps.title: Catalogue the writer's two-handed verdict
steps.text: Critical: 'misleading to imagine Snow as the eagle-eyed anthropologist'; 'descriptions are not exactly subtle'; cultural tribalism 'impaired the possibility of rational discourse'. Positive: 'thoughts about progress are more relevant today than his cultural typologies'; the science-direction question 'speaks most directly to us today'.

steps.title: Identify what the writer salvages
steps.text: The writer rejects Snow's anthropology of two cultures (his typologies are stereotypes), but salvages Snow's PROGRESS-AND-FUTURE arguments — those still earn engagement today.

steps.title: Translate to plain English
steps.text: The article essentially says: Snow's pictures of the two cultures are caricatures, but his question about whether science needs guidance to shape the future is still worth engaging with. The future-oriented bit is the salvageable piece.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.text: Option C: 'Snow's views on humanity's path to the future may still hold some interest.' 'Path to the future' = thoughts on progress and tech; 'may still hold some interest' = the writer's hedged positive verdict. Tight match.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer C. Insight in one line: in mixed-verdict ELF passages, find what the writer EXPLICITLY salvages from a flawed work — that is the answer, not the parts the writer trashes.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to skim the 'eagle-eyed anthropologist' line and miss its negation.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 catches the negation: 'misleading to imagine Snow as the eagle-eyed anthropologist'. The writer rejects his anthropological credentials, not endorses them.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'English physicist' and assume the writer credits Snow's literary insight.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 traces the writer's actual stance: Snow's portraits of literary culture are stereotypes ('not exactly subtle'). The writer never claims his physics background gave him literary insight — he criticises Snow's literary descriptions.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a critique is to assume the writer denies the original divide.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 reads it more carefully: the writer says the SEPARATENESS is 'slippery' but never claims there's no real difference. Option D overshoots into outright denial.

var-2024-verb2-ELF-037.technique: Mixed-verdict ELF questions reward the option naming what the writer SALVAGES from a flawed work. Look for sentences like 'X is dated, yet Y is relevant' — Y is the answer.
var-2024-verb2-ELF-037.pitfall: The cure for the 'all-negative read' trap: even sharply critical articles usually preserve one element of the work being reviewed. Find the 'still relevant', 'continues to', 'most directly speaks' clue and follow it.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the writer pairs 'X is dated' with 'yet Y is more relevant today', pick the option naming Y as the part still worth engaging — that is the writer's actual endorsement.

var-2024-verb2-ELF-038.solution_path: Snow's essay 'provoked a roaring, ad hominem response from the Cambridge literary critic F. R. Leavis and a more measured one from Lionel Trilling. Snow's cultural tribalism, Trilling argued, impaired the possibility of rational discourse.' Two prominent intellectuals attacked the book — one ferociously, one calmly. The reception was rejection. Answer D.

steps.title: What is the question asking?
steps.text: 'Implied' about the RECEPTION of Snow's ideas — meaning how they were received by other thinkers. You need the writer's characterisation of that reception.

steps.title: Locate the reception sentences
steps.text: 'Snow's essay provoked a roaring, ad hominem response from the Cambridge literary critic F. R. Leavis and a more measured one from Lionel Trilling. Snow's cultural tribalism, Trilling argued, impaired the possibility of rational discourse.'

steps.title: Translate the response register
steps.text: 'Roaring, ad hominem' = furious personal attack. 'More measured' but still arguing Snow's tribalism 'impaired the possibility of rational discourse' — that is a critical dismissal in calmer tone. Both responses are rejections; they differ only in volume.

steps.title: Identify who responded
steps.text: Leavis: Cambridge literary critic, intellectually prominent. Trilling: literary critic and major essayist of mid-century. Both are 'prominent intellectuals' — and both rejected Snow's framing.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.text: Option D: 'They were squarely rejected by prominent intellectuals.' 'Squarely rejected' fits roaring ad hominem + measured argument that the book impairs rational discourse; 'prominent intellectuals' fits Leavis and Trilling.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer D. Insight in one line: when an ELF passage describes responses as 'roaring, ad hominem' or arguing the work 'impaired rational discourse', that is rejection by the responders.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'few people actually seem to have read Snow's book' and infer initial neglect.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2 separates the eras: 'few people read it nowadays' is current; the original reception was the roaring response from Leavis. The book was not ignored at first — it was loudly attacked.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the cold-war / scientific framing and assume natural scientists led the argument.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 names the responders: Leavis and Trilling are both LITERARY critics, not natural scientists. The pushback came from the humanities side, exactly the group Snow attacked.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a famous debate is to assume both sides engaged respectfully with the logic.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 catches 'roaring, ad hominem' — that is the explicit opposite of paying due respect to the underlying logic. Leavis attacked Snow personally; Trilling argued Snow's argument was unfit for rational discourse.

var-2024-verb2-ELF-038.technique: When an ELF passage describes a book's reception with words like 'roaring', 'ad hominem', or 'impaired rational discourse', the reception was rejection. Look for option words 'rejected', 'attacked', 'criticised'.
var-2024-verb2-ELF-038.pitfall: The cure for 'initially ignored' trap: 'currently rarely read' is a present-tense fact that doesn't speak to original reception. Anchor your answer to the immediate response Leavis-and-Trilling clause, not the modern-day citation pattern.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the reception is described as 'roaring, ad hominem' from prominent intellectuals, pick the option naming squarely rejection — never the option claiming neutral reception or respectful debate.
